name: Lentils

book_claims.claim: Consumption associated with lower risk of developing cancer and cardiovascular disease
book_claims.mechanism: Second highest protein content of legumes (27%). One of highest concentrations of phenolic compounds and antioxidants among legumes.

supplementary_research.finding: A 2014 meta-analysis (CMAJ, PMID 24871476) of 21 RCTs found that one daily serving of pulses (including lentils) significantly reduced LDL cholesterol by 5%, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction.

The PREDIMED trial secondary analysis (PMID 28011400) found that higher lentil consumption was associated with a 33% lower risk of type 2 diabetes and significantly lower cardiovascular mortality in a Mediterranean population.

A 2018 RCT (J Nutr, PMID 29263222) found that replacing half the available carbohydrate in rice or potatoes with cooked lentils significantly reduced postprandial blood glucose by 20-35%, demonstrating a practical strategy for glycemic control.

A 2017 review (Int J Mol Sci, PMID 28242629) documented lentils as containing the highest phenolic content among common pulses, with condensed tannins, flavonols, and hydroxycinnamic acids that have demonstrated anti-cancer activity in vitro.

culinary_pairings.source: Mujaddara, dal-chawal; complementary amino acids for complete protein
Essential spice in dal, soups, and lentil stews
Acid brightens earthy lentils in soups, stews, and curries
Aromatic base for virtually all lentil preparations
Vitamin C improves iron absorption; acid finishes lentil dishes
Palak dal; complementary iron sources with combined phytonutrients
Anti-inflammatory spice; standard in all Indian dal preparations

seasonality: Dried lentils available year-round. No soaking required unlike most beans. Red and yellow lentils cook in 15-20 minutes; green and brown in 25-35 minutes. French green (Puy) lentils hold shape best.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Second highest protein content of all legumes. High in lysine; combine with grains for complete profile. One cup cooked provides 18g protein.
One cup provides 16g fiber -- over 50% RDA. Mix of soluble, insoluble, and resistant starch.
One cup provides 90% RDA. Critical for DNA synthesis and cardiovascular health.
Non-heme iron; absorption significantly improved by vitamin C (add lemon juice or tomatoes).

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Variable; dark lentils contain more than red/yellow
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highest phenolic content among common pulses. Dark-colored varieties (green, brown, black) contain most. Anti-cancer activity demonstrated in vitro.

synergies.description: Brown rice + lentils provides complete protein with all essential amino acids
Vitamin C from lemon dramatically improves non-heme iron absorption
